rules.title: Win32 App Detection Script Failure
rules.description: Detects when a Win32 app detection script fails or returns unexpected results.
rules.explanation: A Win32 app detection script failed or did not detect the app after installation. This causes the app to appear as 'not installed' despite successful installation. Detection failures that were later resolved by a successful install of the same app are suppressed and do not raise this finding.

remediation.title: Fix detection rules
steps: Review the app's detection rules in Intune
Verify file/registry paths are correct
Test detection script manually on a device

rules.title: App Installation Error
rules.description: Detects app installation failures with specific exit codes or HRESULT error codes and suggests fixes.
rules.explanation: An app installation failed with a specific error code. Common MSI exit codes: 1603 (Fatal error during installation), 1618 (Another installation already in progress), 1619 (Package could not be opened), 1625 (Installation prohibited by policy). Common HRESULT codes are also captured.

remediation.title: Investigate the error code
steps: Check the error code in the rule finding details
Look up the specific error code in Microsoft documentation
Review the IME logs on the device: C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\IntuneManagementExtension\Logs\IntuneManagementExtension.log
Verify all prerequisites for the app are met
Check if another installation was running concurrently (error 1618)

rules.title: App Dependency Chain Failure
rules.description: Detects when an app fails because a dependency app failed to install.
rules.explanation: An app installation failed because one of its dependency apps failed to install. Fix the dependency first, then retry.

remediation.title: Fix dependency chain
steps: Identify which dependency app failed
Fix the dependency app first
Verify dependency chain order in Intune

rules.title: Insufficient Disk Space
rules.description: Detects when disk space is critically low during enrollment, which can cause app installation failures. Confidence increases when app installations actually failed while disk space was low.
rules.explanation:
Performance monitoring shows disk free space dropped below 5 GB during enrollment. This can cause app installation failures, especially for large Win32 apps.

If app installations failed in the same session while free space was low, treat storage exhaustion as the likely contributing cause — the confidence score of this finding is raised accordingly. Verify the individual app failures for their concrete error codes before concluding storage was the only cause.

remediation.title: Free disk space
steps: Check device disk size meets minimum requirements
Clean up temporary files and IME cache (C:\Windows\IMECache)
Consider using a larger disk image

remediation.title: Reduce disk consumption during enrollment
steps: Review the total download size of all blocking apps
Stagger large app installations so temporary install files do not accumulate
Use devices with larger drives for app-heavy deployment profiles

rules.title: Content Download Timeout
rules.description: Detects when app content download times out or fails.
rules.explanation: App content download timed out or failed. This is usually caused by network issues, proxy interference, or CDN problems. The IME log shows a Delivery Optimization timeout or download content error.

remediation.title: Improve download speed
steps: Check network bandwidth
Verify proxy doesn't block *.delivery.mp.microsoft.com
Consider Delivery Optimization configuration

rules.title: App Install Retry Loop
rules.description: Detects when the same app installation is attempted 3 or more times during one enrollment — a retry loop caused by unhonored reboot requests, repeated failures, or detection mismatches.
rules.explanation:
The same app was started 3 or more times during this enrollment. Repeated install attempts have several possible causes:

- **Reboot-required loop** — the app requests a reboot (exit code 3010/1641) that the ESP does not honor, so the install-detect-retry cycle repeats
- **Failure retries** — the install keeps failing and the IME retries it
- **Detection mismatch** — the install succeeds but the detection rule does not match, so the app is retried as 'not installed'

Check the app's install results in this session to see which pattern applies; enrollments that legitimately span multiple reboots can also produce additional install starts.

remediation.title: Identify the retry cause
steps: Review this app's install started/completed/failed events in the session timeline
If reboot-related: check the app's return code mapping includes reboot codes (3010, 1641) and verify ESP reboot behavior settings; consider the 'Hard reboot' return code in Intune
If the install keeps failing: look up the failure error code (see the App Installation Error finding if it fired)
If the install succeeds but is retried: fix the app's detection rule (see the Win32 App Detection Script Failure finding)

rules.title: Multiple App Installation Failures
rules.description: Detects when the app tracking summary reports multiple failed app installations.
rules.explanation: The app tracking summary reports 2 or more failed app installations during enrollment. This suggests a systemic issue rather than an isolated app failure.

remediation.title: Review failed apps
steps: Check the individual app_install_failed events for specific error patterns
Look for a common pattern (all MSI apps, all large apps, all from a specific publisher)
Consider whether a shared prerequisite or dependency is failing

rules.title: Slow App Installation
rules.description: Detects app installations that take longer than 10 minutes.
rules.explanation: At least one app installation took longer than 10 minutes. This can indicate slow content delivery, installation overhead, or endpoint performance bottlenecks.

remediation.title: Analyze slow app behavior
steps: Identify the affected app from matched evidence in the rule result
Check network throughput and proxy impact during app download
Consider moving large/non-critical apps out of ESP blocking scope

rules.title: App Reverted to Error After Successful Install
rules.description: Detects when a specific app (matched by appId) was first reported as successfully installed but subsequently reported an IME enforcement error — while enrollment completed successfully. The app may be broken post-enrollment.
rules.explanation:
An app was initially reported as **successfully installed** (`app_install_completed`) but the IME (Intune Management Extension) subsequently reported an **Error** state for the same app via an `IME-ERROR-REPORT` event — while the overall enrollment completed successfully.

This pattern occurs when the IME re-evaluates enforcement state after the initial install report and detects a failure, e.g. `EnforcementState` transitions from `Success` → `Error`. The affected app and its details are captured in the matched evidence.

Common causes:
- A post-install detection check fails (detection rule mismatch)
- The enforcement error code `-2016345060` (0x87D13B9C) indicates the app state diverged from expected
- The app may appear installed in the ESP summary but is in an error state on the device

**The enrollment succeeded, but the affected app requires attention.**

remediation.title: Investigate the app's post-install enforcement state
steps: Note the appId and appName from the rule finding — these identify the exact affected app
Check the Intune portal: Devices > [Device] > Apps — confirm the current enforcement state of the app
Review IME logs on the device: C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\IntuneManagementExtension\Logs\IntuneManagementExtension.log
Search the log for the appId and look for 'EnforcementState' transitions (Success → Error) near the enrollment time
Error -2016345060 (0x87D13B9C) typically indicates a detection failure after install — verify the app's detection rule in Intune
Trigger a manual sync (Company Portal or Settings > Accounts > Access work or school > Sync) to force re-evaluation

remediation.title: Fix detection or installation issues
steps: If detection fails: correct the detection rule (file path, registry key, or PowerShell script) in the Intune app configuration
If the app itself is broken: redeploy or reassign the app to trigger a fresh install
Consider adding a post-install validation script to catch this scenario proactively

relatedDocs.title: Troubleshoot Win32 app installations
Intune Management Extension logs

rules.title: Enforcement Error Resolved by Successful Detection
rules.description: Detects when an app reported an enforcement error (unmapped exit code) but was subsequently detected as installed. The error is informational — the app installed despite the unmapped exit code.
rules.explanation:
An app installation initially reported an **enforcement error** (unmapped exit code), but the IME detection check subsequently found the app **is installed**.

This happens when the MSI/EXE installer returns a non-standard exit code (e.g., 1603) that has no mapping configured in the Intune app definition. The IME treats unmapped exit codes as errors in its enforcement state, but when detection runs afterward, it confirms the app is present on the device.

**The app is installed and functional.** The enforcement error is an Intune app model artifact, not a real installation failure.

remediation.title: Configure exit code mapping in Intune
steps: Open the Intune portal > Apps > [affected app] > Properties > Edit
In the Return codes section, add a mapping for the reported exit code
Map the exit code to 'Success' or 'Soft reboot' depending on the installer behavior
This prevents the enforcement error from appearing in future deployments

relatedDocs.title: Win32 app return codes in Intune

rules.title: App Detection Failure During ESP (0x87D1041C)
rules.description: Detects when the Enrollment Status Page failed because Intune could not verify an app was installed (HRESULT 0x87D1041C). The installer ran but the detection rule did not match afterwards.

preconditions.description: Scope this rule to sessions where the ESP terminated against the Apps subcategory with the canonical detection-failure HRESULT. Works for any agent version, since esp_failure_settle_started is emitted by the V2 ProvisioningStatusTracker regardless of how the Installing-app promotion is later classified.

rules.explanation:
App **{{appName}}** failed during the Enrollment Status Page with HRESULT **{{errorCode}}** — *Application not detected after installation completed successfully*. The installer reported success but the Intune detection rule (file/registry/script) did not find the app afterwards, so the ESP Apps subcategory failed and the device's enrollment terminated.

This is a confirmed detection-rule mismatch, NOT an install timeout — the install itself most likely completed (or the installer exited 0). Common root causes:

- The detection rule looks for a registry/file path that the installer doesn't create (wrong path, wrong bitness — e.g. checking HKLM\SOFTWARE\... on a 32-bit installer that lands under WOW6432Node).
- The installer writes the detection artefact AFTER the ESP's detection probe runs (e.g. async post-install task, requires reboot).
- The detection script returns 0 with no stdout (Intune treats empty stdout as "not detected").
- The installer wrote files to a user profile but the rule looks in System scope (or vice-versa).

remediation.title: Validate the detection rule against an installed device
steps: Open Intune portal → Apps → {{appName}} → Properties → Detection rules
Note the exact path / registry key / script the rule checks
Install the app manually on a reference device (outside Autopilot) and verify the detection artefact actually exists at that path AFTER the installer finishes
For registry rules: check both HKLM\SOFTWARE\... and HKLM\SOFTWARE\WOW6432Node\... depending on the installer's bitness
For file rules: ensure the path is what the installer actually writes (case, trailing version folder, ProgramData vs Program Files)
For script detection: test the script in PowerShell as SYSTEM (psexec -s) — it must write at least one line to stdout AND exit 0

remediation.title: Investigate post-install delay
steps: Check whether the installer schedules an async task (Windows Installer scheduled reboot, service start) that creates the detection artefact only after ESP completes
If yes: either move the detection artefact creation into the synchronous installer path, or add a wait step in the installer wrapper script before exit
As a workaround, change the detection rule to something the synchronous installer writes immediately (typically the MSI ProductCode under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Uninstall\)

remediation.title: Cross-check the agent's per-app event
steps: Look at the app_install_failed event in this session for DataJson.detectionResult — NotDetected confirms Intune's detection probe ran and returned negative
Look at app_install_failed.bytesDownloaded vs bytesTotal — if they match, the package downloaded fully (install error vs. download error)
Cross-reference DataJson.exitCode and hresultFromWin32 — these come from the installer itself and may explain why the detection artefact was not written

relatedDocs.title: Microsoft Learn: Troubleshoot Windows Autopilot ESP error codes
Win32 app detection rules in Intune

rules.title: MSIX/Store App Failure During ESP (AppX Deployment)
rules.description: Detects when the Enrollment Status Page failed on the Apps subcategory and the agent's AppX deployment log scan identified a suspected MSIX/Store package. Store/MSIX apps install outside the Win32 pipeline, so the regular per-app tracking sees nothing — this correlation names the likely culprit.

preconditions.description: Scope this rule to sessions where the settle-window AppX scan found a package whose deployment error matches the ESP failure HRESULT. Weaker verdicts (appx_activity_no_hresult_match, no_appx_candidates) intentionally do not fire this rule — they carry too little signal for an admin-facing finding.

rules.explanation:
The ESP **{{espCategory}}/Apps** subcategory failed with HRESULT **{{espErrorCode}}** — an error from the AppX/MSIX deployment stack, i.e. a **Microsoft Store / MSIX app**, not a Win32 app. These apps install via the EnterpriseModernAppManagement CSP and never appear in the IME Win32 sidecar logs, which is why all tracked Win32/WinGet apps in this session can look green while ESP still reports an Apps failure.

The agent scanned the AppXDeploymentServer event log during the failure settle window and identified **{{topCandidatePackageName}}** (package `{{topCandidatePackage}}`) as the most likely culprit — its deployment error matches the ESP failure code ({{candidateCount}} candidate(s) total, confidence: {{confidence}}).

**This is a correlation-based assessment, not a confirmed root cause** — validate against the AppXDeploymentServer/Operational event log on the device or in the diagnostics archive before acting.

remediation.title: Confirm the failing package
steps: Open Event Viewer → Applications and Services Logs → Microsoft → Windows → AppXDeploymentServer → Operational on the device (or in the diagnostics archive if uploaded)
Filter for Error-level events around the ESP failure time and confirm the package name and HRESULT match this finding
Look up the HRESULT: 0x80073cf9 = generic install failure, 0x80073cf0 = invalid/corrupt package, 0x80073cff = sideloading/licensing policy block, 0x80073d02 = blocked because app is in use

remediation.title: Check Store app assignment and licensing
steps: Intune portal → Apps → check the assignment of the suspected app: device context vs user context — user-context Store apps installing during Account Setup ESP are a common failure source
Verify the app's license type (online vs offline): online-licensed Store apps require the user's Entra ID token and Store connectivity during ESP
Check whether the Microsoft Store endpoints are reachable from the enrollment network (proxy/firewall allowlists)
If the app is not business-critical during provisioning: remove it from the ESP blocking-app list so a failure no longer terminates enrollment

remediation.title: Consider repackaging
steps: For maximum ESP reliability, wrap the app as a Win32 (.intunewin) deployment instead of a Store/MSIX assignment — Win32 installs are fully tracked and produce per-app failure telemetry
Alternatively deploy the app post-enrollment (available, not required) so it cannot block the ESP

relatedDocs.title: Microsoft Learn: Troubleshoot Windows Autopilot ESP error codes
Add Microsoft Store apps to Intune

rules.title: Proxy Configuration Causing Download Failure
rules.description: Correlates an active proxy configuration with app content download failures (Delivery Optimization timeout or download error), suggesting the proxy is blocking or throttling Intune content delivery.
rules.explanation:
A proxy (explicit or PAC-based) is configured on this device and app content downloads failed (Delivery Optimization timeout or download content error). Proxies can block or throttle downloads from *.delivery.mp.microsoft.com and other Intune CDN endpoints.

This is a common root cause when app downloads fail consistently on proxy-connected corporate networks. Note this rule only fires when the failure is download-related — generic app install errors behind a proxy do not trigger it.

remediation.title: Verify proxy bypass for Intune endpoints
steps: Add *.delivery.mp.microsoft.com to proxy bypass list
Add *.do.dsp.mp.microsoft.com (Delivery Optimization) to bypass list
Add login.microsoftonline.com and *.manage.microsoft.com to bypass list
Verify the PAC file is reachable at enrollment time (before user login)

remediation.title: Check proxy authentication
steps: Ensure the proxy does not require user authentication during OOBE/device phase
Consider using machine certificate-based proxy authentication
Test with proxy temporarily bypassed to confirm it is the root cause

rules.title: Windows Hello Provisioning Timeout
rules.description: Detects when Windows Hello for Business provisioning did not start within the expected timeframe after ESP completion.
rules.explanation: Windows Hello for Business provisioning did not start within the expected time after ESP exit. This may indicate that the Hello policy is not configured, the device doesn't meet prerequisites, or there's a connectivity issue with the Key Registration Service.

remediation.title: Verify Windows Hello configuration
steps: Check if Windows Hello for Business is configured in the tenant
Verify the policy targets the device/user correctly
Ensure the device has a TPM 2.0 (required for WHfB)
Check connectivity to the Key Registration Service (enterpriseregistration.windows.net)

rules.title: Sustained High Memory Usage During Enrollment
rules.description: Detects when memory usage stays above 90% across at least 3 performance snapshots during enrollment, indicating sustained memory pressure that can cause app installation failures or system instability.
rules.explanation: Memory usage exceeded 90% in at least 3 performance snapshots during this enrollment — sustained pressure, not a single transient spike. Persistent high memory usage can cause app installation failures, system slowdowns, and ESP timeouts, especially on devices with 8 GB RAM installing several apps concurrently.

remediation.title: Reduce memory pressure
steps: Review which apps are being installed concurrently during ESP
Consider staggering resource-heavy installations
Check minimum RAM requirements for the device (8 GB recommended)
Investigate if background services are consuming excessive memory

rules.title: Unsupported Windows Version
rules.description: Detects devices enrolling with an OS build that has reached end of support: Windows 11 22H2 (build 22621) or older, which includes every Windows 10 build. Maintenance note: raise the build cutoff to 22631 after Windows 11 23H2 reaches end of support on 2026-11-10.
rules.explanation:
This device is running an OS build that has reached end of support:

- **OS:** `{{osVersion}}`
- **Build:** `{{currentBuild}}`

Builds up to 22621 cover Windows 11 22H2 and older as well as every Windows 10 release — Microsoft no longer provides security updates or bug fixes for these versions (exception: LTSC/IoT LTSC editions follow their own lifecycle; verify the edition if this device is LTSC). Devices running unsupported OS versions are exposed to known vulnerabilities and may fail compliance checks.

remediation.title: Update to a supported Windows 11 version
steps: Verify the device meets hardware requirements for Windows 11 23H2 or 24H2
Update the Windows Autopilot deployment profile to use a supported OS image (23H2 or later)
If using a custom OS image, rebuild it with a supported Windows 11 version
For existing devices, deploy a Windows 11 feature update via Intune or Windows Update for Business

relatedDocs.title: Windows 11 release information
Windows lifecycle (Home and Pro)

rules.title: Windows Update Failed During Enrollment
rules.description: Detects a Windows quality/cumulative update that FAILED to install during the OOBE / ESP enrollment window (WindowsUpdateClient EventID 20). A cumulative update failing mid-OOBE is a known cause of enrollment errors and is otherwise invisible in the Intune console.
rules.explanation: A Windows Update failed to install during enrollment: **{{updateTitle}}** (error {{hresult}} {{hresultSymbol}}). Windows can install quality/cumulative updates during OOBE (the "Getting updates" screen and the ESP "Install Windows quality updates during OOBE" option). When one of these fails mid-enrollment it can leave the device in an inconsistent state, force an unexpected reboot, or block the Enrollment Status Page — a failure mode that is otherwise only discoverable by deep manual digging on the device.

remediation.title: Investigate the failed update
steps: Decode the HRESULT ({{hresult}}) against the Windows Update error reference to identify the failure class (WU agent, CBS/servicing, or network).
Check whether the same KB fails on other devices — a broadly failing cumulative update points at a servicing-stack or content issue rather than a single device.
Consider whether Windows Update during OOBE should be deferred (Autopilot ESP setting / update ring) until the problematic update is superseded.
Confirm the device reached the desktop despite the failure; a failed OOBE update does not always fail the enrollment but frequently correlates with ESP delays.

relatedDocs.title: Windows Update error reference

rules.title: Windows Update Installed During Enrollment
rules.description: Informational: a Windows quality/cumulative update installed during the OOBE / ESP enrollment window (WindowsUpdateClient EventID 19). Surfaces updates applied mid-enrollment — useful context when correlating enrollment duration, reboots, or later issues with a specific KB.
rules.explanation: One or more Windows updates installed during enrollment (e.g. **{{updateTitle}}**). This is normal when Windows Update runs during OOBE, but it extends enrollment time and can trigger a reboot mid-ESP. Track it when correlating enrollment duration or reboot count with a specific cumulative update, or when a device behaves differently after a particular KB landed during provisioning.

remediation.title: Optional review
steps: No action required for a successful install — this is informational.
If enrollment time or reboot count is a concern, review whether quality updates during OOBE are desired for your Autopilot profile.
Correlate the installed KB with any post-enrollment issues if the device behaves unexpectedly.

rules.title: OS Build Changed During Enrollment
rules.description: Informational: the OS build (CurrentBuild.UBR) changed across a reboot during enrollment — an update was installed, regardless of which servicing path delivered it. Deterministic corroboration that works even when the Windows Update event channel shows nothing (registry comparison across restart cannot be lost to timing or logging-path differences).
rules.explanation: The OS build changed from **{{previousBuild}}** to **{{currentBuild}}** across a reboot during enrollment — a Windows update was installed mid-provisioning. This is normal when quality updates run during OOBE, but it adds a reboot and extends enrollment time. If a windows_update_channel_census event is also present, the update installed via a servicing path the Windows Update watcher does not capture yet — its EventID histogram tells which IDs to enable.

remediation.title: Optional review
steps: No action required — this is informational context for enrollment duration and reboot analysis.
Correlate the build jump with reboots and any behavior change after enrollment.
If quality updates during OOBE are not desired for this Autopilot profile, review the ESP 'Install quality updates during OOBE' setting.

rules.title: System Clock Skew — TPM Attestation and ESP Timeout Risk
rules.description: The device's real-time clock deviates from NTP by more than two minutes. Microsoft documents that a clock off by several minutes causes TPM attestation errors (self-deploying/pre-provisioning) and ESP timeouts. Knowledge source: docs/agent/autopilot-ztd-diagnostics.md.
rules.author: Autopilot Monitor
rules.explanation:
The device's system clock is off by **{{offsetSeconds}} seconds** relative to the NTP reference (time.windows.com). Microsoft documents that Autopilot provisioning can fail with TPM attestation errors or ESP timeouts when the real-time clock is off by several minutes — certificate and token validation are time-sensitive.

This is especially critical for **self-deploying and pre-provisioning** deployments (TPM attestation) and can also skew all client-side timestamps in this session's timeline.

remediation.title: Sync the clock before provisioning
steps: Boot the device to the start of OOBE and establish a network connection.
Run 'w32tm /resync /force' (Shift+F10 command prompt) to sync with time.windows.com.
Ensure UDP port 123 to time.windows.com is open in the deployment network.
Check the BIOS/RTC battery and firmware clock if the skew recurs on the same hardware.

relatedDocs.title: Microsoft Learn: Windows Autopilot known issues — device provisioning can fail (clock skew)
Microsoft Learn: Windows Autopilot requirements — Networking (NTP)

rules.title: Enrollment Failed
rules.description: Detects when the Autopilot enrollment has explicitly failed.
rules.explanation:
The Autopilot enrollment has explicitly failed.

**Key facts extracted from this session's events:**

- **Failure reason:** `{{reason}}`
- **First failed app:** `{{appName}}`
- **ESP failed subcategory:** `{{failedSubcategory}}`
- **ESP HRESULT:** `{{errorCode}}`

When an ESP Apps-subcategory failure is paired with HRESULT `0x87D1041C`, the device-side install most likely completed but the Intune detection rule did not match afterwards — see the **App Detection Failure During ESP** rule (ANALYZE-APP-013) in this same analysis for the actionable root cause and remediation steps. Any field still shown as a literal `{{…}}` placeholder was simply not recorded for this session — for example, a non-ESP failure path has no ESP failed-subcategory or HRESULT.

remediation.title: Investigate enrollment failure
steps: Start with the more specific ANALYZE-APP-* / ANALYZE-ESP-* rules that fired alongside this one — they correlate specific HRESULTs to actionable causes (e.g. 0x87D1041C → detection rule mismatch in ANALYZE-APP-013)
Read the `reason` field on the enrollment_failed event for the immediate trigger (e.g. esp_terminal_failure, hello_timeout, max_lifetime)
If a HRESULT is present, look it up against the ESP error catalog (Microsoft Learn: Autopilot ESP error codes)
Review other analyze rule results for correlated issues (disk space, network, app failures)
Check the device event timeline for the sequence of events leading to failure
Verify the Autopilot profile and ESP configuration in Intune

rules.title: Session Timed Out
rules.description: Detects when the enrollment session was force-failed by the server-side inactivity timeout (no agent telemetry for the configured SessionTimeoutHours window) without ever completing or explicitly failing.

preconditions.description: Suppress when the agent later reported a successful completion. The 5h server timeout can fire before the agent's own 6h max-lifetime, so a slow-but-successful session may complete after the sweep marked it timed out.
Suppress when an explicit enrollment_failed exists — that path is already covered by ANALYZE-ENRL-001; avoid double-reporting the same session.

rules.explanation:
The enrollment session was force-failed by the server-side maintenance sweep after `{{timeoutHours}}` hour(s) of agent inactivity — the device stopped sending telemetry and never reported completion or an explicit failure.

This usually means the agent process died or the device went offline mid-enrollment (power off, network loss, bluescreen, reimage) rather than a clean enrollment failure. Because there is no terminal `enrollment_failed` event, the more specific app/ESP rules cannot pinpoint a cause — the evidence simply stops at the last event before the gap.

remediation.title: Investigate the silent session
steps: Open the device event timeline and look at the LAST events before the gap — the final phase / app reached is where the device went silent
Check whether the device is still enrolled and online in Intune; a reimaged or powered-off device explains the silence
Look for previous_crash_detected / agent_shutting_down events earlier in the session that hint at an agent or OS crash
If the device is reachable, collect the agent + IME logs and re-run enrollment, correlating with the timeout timestamp
If many sessions time out in the same window, check for a tenant-wide network/connectivity issue or an Intune service incident

rules.title: TPM Attestation Failure — Known Error Code
rules.description: A TPM-attestation-related error code documented in the Microsoft Autopilot known-issues list was observed (0x800705B4, 0x801C03EA, 0x81039001, 0x81039023, 0x81039024, 0x80070490). These block self-deploying and pre-provisioning deployments during 'Securing your hardware'. Observability: attestation precedes MDM enrollment, so in the normal deployment model (agent installed via IME after enrollment) these codes are rarely visible — the rule primarily serves bootstrap-token deployments. See docs/agent/autopilot-ztd-diagnostics.md (Observability section).
rules.author: Autopilot Monitor
rules.explanation:
A TPM attestation error code from Microsoft's Autopilot known-issues list was observed. These codes block self-deploying mode and pre-provisioning during the **Securing your hardware** step.

Code meanings:

- **0x800705B4** — timeout; the most common cause in self-deploying mode is hardware that is not TPM 2.0 capable (e.g. a virtual machine). Self-deploying mode cannot be used on such devices.
- **0x801C03EA** — TPM attestation failed, so the Microsoft Entra join with a device token failed.
- **0x81039001** — E_AUTOPILOT_CLIENT_TPM_MAX_ATTESTATION_RETRY_EXCEEDED; intermittent — a retry of the deployment often succeeds.
- **0x81039023** — Windows 11 attestation failure; fixed by cumulative update KB5013943 (21H2) or later.
- **0x81039024** — the TPM firmware has known vulnerabilities; attestation is refused until the firmware is updated.
- **0x80070490** — AMD platforms with ASP firmware TPM; fixed in later AMD firmware releases.

TPM attestation additionally requires access to `*.microsoftaik.azure.net` and, for firmware TPMs, the vendor EK-certificate endpoints. A real-time clock that is off by several minutes also causes attestation failures (see the session's ntp_time_check event).

remediation.title: Match the code to its documented fix
steps: 0x800705B4: verify the device has TPM 2.0 (self-deploying/pre-provisioning is unsupported on VMs).
0x81039001: retry the deployment — this code is documented as intermittent.
0x81039023: install KB5013943 (Windows 11 21H2) or a later cumulative update into the image.
0x81039024 / 0x80070490: update the TPM/system firmware from the OEM.
Verify network access to *.microsoftaik.azure.net and the TPM vendor EK-certificate URL (Intel/Qualcomm/AMD).
Check the session's ntp_time_check event — a clock offset of minutes breaks attestation (w32tm /resync /force).

relatedDocs.title: Microsoft Learn: Windows Autopilot known issues
Microsoft Learn: Windows Autopilot requirements — Networking (TPM attestation endpoints)

rules.title: MDM Enrollment Blocked — Known Error Code
rules.description: An enrollment-blocking error code documented in the Microsoft Autopilot known-issues/troubleshooting pages was observed (0x80180014, 0x80180018, 0xC1036501, 0x801C03F3, 0x80070774). Each has a specific, documented cause and fix. Observability: a blocked MDM enrollment normally means no agent gets installed at all (IME install happens after enrollment) — this rule can realistically only fire for bootstrap-token deployments (pre-MDM reporting) or mixed retry scenarios. See docs/agent/autopilot-ztd-diagnostics.md (Observability section).
rules.author: Autopilot Monitor
rules.explanation:
An enrollment-blocking error code from Microsoft's Autopilot known-issues list was observed.

Code meanings:

- **0x80180014** — either the device is being reused in self-deploying/pre-provisioning mode and its Intune-created device record still exists (documented ETW companion error: "Enrollment blocked for AP device by SDM One Time Limit Check"), or Windows (MDM) enrollment is disabled by an enrollment restriction.
- **0x80180018** — MDM enrollment refused for the user: missing/incorrect Intune, EMS, or Microsoft 365 license, or the user exceeded the device enrollment limit.
- **0xC1036501** — automatic MDM enrollment is impossible because multiple MDM configurations exist in Microsoft Entra ID.
- **0x801C03F3** — the Microsoft Entra device object for this Autopilot device was deleted (logged in the Microsoft-Windows-User Device Registration/Admin channel). The object is Autopilot's anchor for group membership and profile targeting.
- **0x80070774** — hybrid-join ESP failure caused by a domain mismatch: the Intune Connector for Active Directory is installed in a different AD domain than the one the device is targeted at.

remediation.title: Match the code to its documented fix
steps: 0x80180014 (reuse): Intune → Devices → Enrollment → Windows Autopilot → Devices → select the device → 'Unblock device', then redeploy. Alternatively verify Windows (MDM) is set to Allow in the enrollment restrictions.
0x80180018: verify the signing-in user has a valid Intune/EMS/M365 license and has not hit the device enrollment limit.
0xC1036501: consolidate to a single MDM configuration in Microsoft Entra ID.
0x801C03F3: deregister the device from Intune, Entra ID, and Autopilot, then re-register it — this recreates the Entra device object.
0x80070774: install/configure the Intune Connector for Active Directory in the AD domain that matches the device targeting.

relatedDocs.title: Microsoft Learn: Windows Autopilot troubleshooting FAQ
Microsoft Learn: Windows Autopilot known issues

rules.title: Hybrid Join Timeout 0x80004005 — Fixed by Windows Update
rules.description: A Microsoft Entra hybrid join Autopilot deployment hit error 0x80004005 — a documented build-dependent known issue, resolved by KB5065789 (25H2, build >= 26200.6725), KB5065426 (24H2, build >= 26100.6584), and KB5070312 (23H2, build >= 22631.6276). Knowledge source: docs/agent/autopilot-ztd-diagnostics.md (error-code map).
rules.author: Autopilot Monitor
rules.explanation:
This is a **Microsoft Entra hybrid join** deployment that hit error **0x80004005** — a documented, build-dependent Autopilot known issue where hybrid deployments time out.

The issue is fixed by Windows cumulative updates:

- Windows 11 **25H2**: KB5065789 — fixed from build **26200.6725**
- Windows 11 **24H2**: KB5065426 — fixed from build **26100.6584**
- Windows 11 **23H2**: KB5070312 — fixed from build **22631.6276**

Compare this session's OS build (see the Session Info card / os_info event) against the fixed-build threshold for its version. If the device booted with an older build, updating the deployment image (or installing quality updates during OOBE) resolves the timeout. Note that 0x80004005 is a generic failure code (E_FAIL) — if the build is already at or above the fixed threshold, investigate the Intune Connector for Active Directory and domain-controller connectivity instead.

remediation.title: Update the image to the fixed build
steps: Determine the session's OS build from the Session Info card (e.g. 26100.4500).
Refresh the deployment image with the current cumulative update, or enable quality updates during OOBE in the ESP profile.
If the build already contains the fix: check Intune Connector for AD health, domain-controller reachability from the client network, and hybrid-join prerequisites.

relatedDocs.title: Microsoft Learn: Windows Autopilot known issues — hybrid join 0x80004005

rules.title: ESP Blocking App Timeout
rules.description: Detects when the DeviceSetup phase of the ESP took longer than 30 minutes, indicating a stuck or very slow blocking app. Confidence increases when the phase exceeds 60 minutes.
rules.explanation: The ESP DeviceSetup phase took more than 30 minutes. This indicates a blocking app is stuck installing, downloading very slowly, or has entered a retry loop. A duration beyond 60 minutes raises the confidence further — at that point the enrollment is almost certainly held by a single stuck blocking item rather than ordinary slowness.

remediation.title: Investigate blocking app
steps: Identify which app is blocking (check ESP events)
Verify app content is downloadable
Consider reducing the number of blocking apps

rules.title: ESP Subcategory Failed (Certificates)
rules.description: Detects when the Certificates subcategory of the Enrollment Status Page reported a failure, even if the enrollment later appears to have succeeded. Users can click 'Continue anyway' on an ESP error, which masks the underlying certificate provisioning failure — this rule surfaces the transition so it stays visible.
rules.author: Autopilot Monitor
rules.explanation:
The ESP Certificates subcategory transitioned to **failed** during enrollment. This is frequently masked when the user clicks 'Continue anyway' on the ESP error screen — the overall enrollment then appears successful while a required certificate (e.g. Wi-Fi, VPN, SCEP/PKCS) was never deployed.

The subcategory name is read from the registry (`CertificatesSubcategory`) and is language-invariant, so this rule fires regardless of OS display language.

remediation.title: Validate certificate delivery on the device
steps: Run `certutil -store My` and `certutil -store -user My` on the device to inspect installed certificates
Check `Event Viewer → Applications and Services Logs → Microsoft → Windows → DeviceManagement-Enterprise-Diagnostics-Provider → Admin` for SCEP/PKCS errors
Review the Intune portal for the device's certificate profile assignments and deployment status

remediation.title: Fix common certificate provisioning issues
steps: Verify the NDES/SCEP connector is online and healthy
Confirm the certificate template permissions grant 'Enroll' to the Intune service account / NDES computer account
Check that the device has network line-of-sight to the SCEP endpoint at the time of ESP
Re-sync the device (Settings → Accounts → Access work or school → Info → Sync) and re-check the Certificates subcategory

relatedDocs.title: Microsoft Learn: Troubleshoot certificate profiles in Intune
Microsoft Learn: Enrollment Status Page tracking

rules.title: ESP Timeout with 'Continue Anyway' (Soft Failure)
rules.description: Detects an ESP terminal failure on a profile that allows 'Continue anyway'. The enrollment is reported as failed, but the user most likely dismissed the error screen and reached the desktop — a soft failure rather than an unusable device.
rules.explanation:
This enrollment was reported as **failed** because the Enrollment Status Page reached its configured terminal timeout (`{{espSyncFailureTimeoutMinutes}} min`) before all blocking items finished. The ESP profile, however, allows **'Continue anyway'** — so the user most likely dismissed the error screen and reached the desktop with a working, though not fully provisioned, device.

The agent only observes the ESP terminal failure on the device side; it cannot see what the user did after the failure screen. Treat this as a **soft failure**: the device may well be in productive use, while at least one blocking item (`{{failedSubcategory}}` subcategory / app `{{appName}}`) did not complete inside the ESP window.

A cluster of these across a tenant almost always points at one slow blocking app holding the whole fleet at the ESP timeout wall, not at broken devices. Any field still shown as a literal `{{…}}` placeholder was simply not recorded for this session (e.g. a certificate-only failure has no failed app).

remediation.title: Relieve ESP pressure so enrollments finish inside the window
steps: Identify the slow blocking app (`{{appName}}`) and either remove it from the ESP 'block device use until these required apps are installed' list, or raise the ESP installation timeout (currently {{espSyncFailureTimeoutMinutes}} min).
If the app legitimately needs longer than the ESP allows, make it non-blocking and let it install after the desktop arrives.
Verify the app's detection rule matches after install — an app that installs but fails detection times out the ESP on every device (see ANALYZE-APP-013).

If a subcategory such as Certificates is reported under '{{failedSubcategory}}', confirm the certificate/connector actually delivers — 'Continue anyway' hides a missing certificate, it does not fix it.

rules.title: MDM Policy Forced a Mid-ESP Reboot (Second Sign-In)
rules.description: Attributes an observed enrollment reboot to device-assigned MDM policies that Windows flagged as reboot-required. Fires ONLY when both facts are present in the session: at least one actually-observed reboot (system_reboot_detected) AND reboot-required policy flags (mdm_policy_reboot_required). Policy flags alone — e.g. from a user-policy sync during AccountSetup — force no reboot and stay advisory-silent.
rules.author: Autopilot Monitor
rules.explanation:
This session **rebooted during enrollment**, and Windows flagged one or more device-assigned MDM policies as **reboot-required** — the classic cause of the coalesced mid-ESP restart and the unexpected **second sign-in screen** before AccountSetup.

First reboot-forcing policy URI: `{{firstRebootUri}}`

The complete URI list is in the `mdm_policy_reboot_required` timeline event (`rebootUris`). Policies applied DURING ESP DeviceSetup are coalesced into one forced restart at the end of DeviceSetup; the same flags raised later (e.g. user-policy sync in AccountSetup) have no immediate effect — this advisory only fires when a reboot was actually observed.

This is expected OS behavior, not a failure — but it degrades the enrollment experience, confuses users, and is invisible in the Intune console.

remediation.title: Reassign the reboot-forcing policies to user groups
steps: Open the `mdm_policy_reboot_required` timeline event and note the CSP URIs in `rebootUris` (e.g. `./Device/Vendor/MSFT/...`)
Map each URI to its Intune profile (Settings Catalog / configuration profile) — the URI path names the CSP area
Reassign those profiles from device groups to user groups so they apply during/after AccountSetup instead of DeviceSetup
Alternatively, exclude the Autopilot device group from the profile and deliver the setting post-enrollment

remediation.title: Or accept the reboot and set expectations
steps: If the policy must be device-assigned (e.g. a security baseline), keep it and document the expected mid-ESP restart
Communicate to users that a restart and second sign-in during provisioning is expected for these deployments

relatedDocs.title: Patch My PC: Avoid the second sign-in screen after Autopilot
Microsoft Learn: Enrollment Status Page tracking

rules.title: ESP Failure Recovered After User Retry
rules.description: Detects the recovery story: the ESP reported a terminal failure (typically DeviceSetup/Apps), the user most likely pressed 'Try again' on the failure page, the failed step re-ran and completed successfully. The earlier failure did not stick — the enrollment continued past it.
rules.explanation:
The Enrollment Status Page reported a **failure** in the `{{category}}` phase (`{{failedSubcategory}}` subcategory, most likely app(s): `{{likelyCulpritApps}}`) — and then **recovered**: the failed step left its failed state and completed successfully about `{{minutesSinceFailure}}` min later.

That pattern is consistent with the user pressing **'Try again'** on the ESP failure page (the profile allows failure-page actions; the agent cannot observe the click itself). The failure the user saw was real, but it did not stick — the ESP re-ran the step, it succeeded, and the enrollment continued.

Read the session timeline as one arc: `esp_failure_advisory` (the failure, with the likely culprit app) → `esp_failure_retry_detected` (the retry) → `esp_failure_recovered` (the recovery). If this pattern repeats across devices for the same app, that app's install is flaky on first attempt — fix the app rather than the devices. Any literal `{{…}}` placeholder means that detail was not recorded for this session.

remediation.title: Make the first install attempt stick
steps: Check the likely culprit app(s) (`{{likelyCulpritApps}}`) in Intune: an app that fails on the first ESP pass but succeeds on retry usually has a dependency/ordering or detection-timing problem.
If the app shows 'never started installing' in esp_failure_settle_started, verify its assignment and applicability rules — ESP counted it as required, but IME never processed it.
If the pattern repeats across the tenant, consider removing the app from the ESP blocking list until the first-attempt install is reliable — users are seeing an avoidable failure screen.
No device-side action is needed for this session: the failure recovered and the enrollment continued.

rules.title: Expected Machine Certificate Not Found
rules.description: Detects when a certificate with the configured subject is missing from the local machine personal store (LocalMachine\My). Requires the gather rule GATHER-ID-002 (machine cert store inventory) to be enabled — the check runs against its gather_machine_cert_store output. A missing certificate may indicate that MDM enrollment certificate provisioning or SCEP certificate deployment failed.
rules.explanation: The expected certificate with the configured subject was not found in the local machine certificate store (LocalMachine\My). Customize the 'value' field in the 'cert_subject_missing' condition to match your expected certificate subject (e.g. 'CN=Contoso Root CA'). The check is case-insensitive (substring match). This rule depends on the gather rule GATHER-ID-002 delivering the machine cert store inventory (gather_machine_cert_store event) — without it the rule never evaluates. If the certificate should have been provisioned via SCEP or MDM enrollment, this may indicate a deployment failure.

remediation.title: Verify the expected certificate
steps: Customize the analyze rule: change 'CN=YOUR-CERTIFICATE-SUBJECT' to your expected certificate subject (case-insensitive substring match)
Example subjects: 'CN=Contoso SCEP Cert', 'CN=Contoso Root CA', 'CN=MDM Device Certificate'
Check certlm.msc (Certificates - Local Computer > Personal > Certificates) manually to see what's present

remediation.title: Troubleshoot missing certificate
steps: Check if the device is properly enrolled in Intune (dsregcmd /status)
Verify SCEP/PKCS certificate profiles are assigned and targeted correctly
Check the NDES/SCEP connector status and reachability
Review IntuneManagementExtension logs for certificate enrollment errors
Ensure prerequisite certificates (Trusted Root CA) are deployed before the end-entity profile

templateVariables.name: cert_subject
templateVariables.label: Certificate Subject
templateVariables.description: The expected certificate subject in your environment (e.g., 'CN=Contoso Root CA'). Case-insensitive substring match against the local machine certificate store.

rules.title: Unexpected Local Admin Accounts Detected
rules.description: Detects when the LocalAdminAnalyzer flags unexpected local user accounts or profile directories on the device. This is a known Autopilot bypass technique where admin accounts are created before or during enrollment to gain persistent local admin access.
rules.author: Autopilot Monitor
rules.explanation:
The agent detected unexpected local user accounts or profile directories on this device that are not in the built-in or tenant-configured allowed list.

This is a security-relevant finding. Creating local admin accounts before or during Autopilot enrollment is a known bypass technique (e.g. via BypassNRO or Shift+F10 during OOBE) that grants persistent local administrator access.

Click the event link below to jump to the local_admin_analysis event and check: unexpected_accounts (local user accounts not on the allowed list), unexpected_profiles (C:\Users profile directories not on the allowed list), bypass_nro (whether the BypassNRO registry flag was set), and confidence_score (the agent's confidence in the finding, 0-100).

If these accounts are expected for your environment (e.g. a service account or helpdesk account), add them to the allowed list in Tenant Settings.

remediation.title: Review the unexpected accounts
steps: Open the session timeline and locate the local_admin_analysis event
Check the 'unexpected_accounts' and 'unexpected_profiles' lists in the event data
Verify whether these accounts are legitimate for your environment

remediation.title: Add expected accounts to the allowed list (false positive)
steps: Go to Settings → Agent Analyzers section
Under 'Allowed Admin Accounts', add the account names that are expected in your environment
Save the settings — future enrollments will no longer flag these accounts

remediation.title: Investigate a potential security issue
steps: Check if the BypassNRO registry flag was set (indicates OOBE was bypassed)
Review the enrollment timeline for Shift+F10 or other OOBE escape indicators
Check whether the unexpected account has admin group membership
Consider re-imaging the device if unauthorized admin access is confirmed

rules.title: Critical Vulnerability Detected
rules.description: Fires when the vulnerability correlation finds critical-severity CVEs or CISA KEV entries in installed software. This indicates the device has software with known, actively exploited vulnerabilities.
rules.author: Autopilot Monitor
rules.explanation: The device has installed software with critical vulnerabilities. Software with CVEs rated CRITICAL (CVSS >= 9.0) or listed in the CISA Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog was detected. These vulnerabilities are actively targeted by attackers and should be remediated immediately.

remediation.title: Review vulnerability findings
steps: Open the Vulnerability Report section in the session detail
Identify all software with critical or KEV-flagged vulnerabilities
Check if newer versions are available that address these CVEs
Update the affected software in your Intune app deployment

remediation.title: Update deployment packages
steps: Check Intune for outdated app package versions
Update the deployment package to the latest patched version
Re-deploy to affected devices

relatedDocs.title: CISA KEV Catalog
NVD - National Vulnerability Database

rules.title: Microsoft 365 Apps Install Failed
rules.description: Detects when the Microsoft 365 Apps (Office Click-to-Run) background install did not reach completion. The V2 OfficeInstallDetector watches the C2R client directly (registry + process) and emits office_install_failed when the install scenario was abandoned (worker gone and streaming never finished) or a C2R failure code was observed. This catches Office failures that the Intune integrated app type hides, because IME reports the app done within 1-2 minutes while C2R keeps installing in the background.
rules.explanation:
The Microsoft 365 Apps (Office Click-to-Run) install did not finish.

The OfficeInstallDetector watches the C2R client directly and reports the real background install lifecycle. The Intune "integrated" Microsoft 365 Apps app type only pushes the configuration XML and starts the C2R bootstrapper, so IME marks the app done within 1-2 minutes while C2R keeps streaming and laying down the product for several more minutes. This event means C2R activity ended (the OfficeC2RClient worker exited and the install scenario cleared) without the StreamingFinished flag being set, or a C2R failure code was observed.

Review the office_install_failed event payload: `products`, `channel`, `phase`, `streamingFinished`, `errorCode` (best-effort — may be empty) and `durationSeconds`.

remediation.title: Investigate the Office C2R install failure
steps: Open the office_install_failed event and review products, channel, phase, streamingFinished and errorCode
Check the Click-to-Run logs on the device (%windir%\Temp and %temp%, files named <MachineName>-yyyymmdd-nnnn.log) for the failing operation
Verify the device had network reachability to the Office CDN / Microsoft Connected Cache during the enrollment window
Confirm the Intune Microsoft 365 Apps app configuration (products, channel, architecture) matches what the device can install
Re-check the session timeline for a reboot or agent shutdown that may have interrupted the background C2R install

rules.title: Secure Boot UEFI CA 2023 certificate not deployed
rules.description: Detects devices where the Windows UEFI CA 2023 certificate has not been deployed to the Secure Boot DB. The 2011 certificates expired in June 2026 — affected devices no longer receive Secure Boot security updates until the 2023 certificate is deployed. Since v3 the agent reads the firmware db/KEK variables directly: when the firmware itself confirms the 2023 certificate, the rule is suppressed even if the Windows servicing status has not caught up yet.

preconditions.description: Skip on virtual machines — Secure Boot DB is host-controlled (Hyper-V/VMware/etc), the inside-the-VM UEFICA2023Status registry value is not the right indicator and not actionable for the VM admin.

Skip when the firmware db itself confirms the Windows UEFI CA 2023 certificate — the marker is only emitted on positive firmware confirmation, so old agents (field absent) and unconfirmed devices keep evaluating the rule. The servicing registry status lags behind actual firmware state (OEM factory image, WinCS, manual deployment, or WU simply not run yet).

rules.explanation:
This device has Secure Boot enabled but the **Windows UEFI CA 2023** certificate has not been deployed to the Secure Boot DB.

The original Windows UEFI CA 2011 certificates **expired on June 27, 2026 — this deadline has passed**. The automatic pre-expiry rollout window is over: this device no longer receives Secure Boot security updates (DB/DBX) and cannot trust 2023-signed boot managers until the 2023 certificate is deployed. Deploying it now requires active remediation (Windows servicing opt-in and, on some models, an OEM firmware update) rather than waiting for an automatic update.

On devices running a current agent this finding is verified against the firmware itself: the agent reads the UEFI `db` signature database directly, and this warning is automatically suppressed when the firmware already contains the 2023 certificate — regardless of the Windows servicing status below.

Current UEFI CA 2023 servicing status: `{{uefiCA2023Status}}`

remediation.title: Deploy UEFI CA 2023 certificate now
steps: Deploy KB5036210 via Windows Update or WSUS
Use Intune policy to enable Secure Boot DB update (Windows UEFI CA 2023)
Check with the device OEM whether a firmware update is required for the DB update to apply on this model
Alternatively, use the WinCS CLI tool from Microsoft for manual deployment

Verify deployment against the firmware directly: [Text.Encoding]::ASCII.GetString((Get-SecureBootUEFI -Name db).Bytes).Contains('Windows UEFI CA 2023') — this is authoritative; the registry value HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\SecureBoot\Servicing\UEFICA2023Status = 'Updated' only reflects the Windows Update rollout
After DB update: ensure the boot manager is updated to a 2023-signed version via monthly cumulative update

relatedDocs.title: Microsoft: Secure Boot certificates expired June 2026 — act now
KB5036210: Deploying Windows UEFI CA 2023

rules.title: AutoLogon plaintext password stored in registry
rules.description: Fires when the AutoLogon analyzer reports a DefaultPassword value present in Winlogon. A plaintext AutoLogon password stored in the registry is readable by any local administrator and is a credential exposure — more severe than AutoLogon alone (the Sysinternals Autologon tool deliberately avoids this by storing the secret in the LSA instead).
rules.explanation:
This device has a **plaintext AutoLogon password stored in the registry** (`DefaultPassword` under `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon`). This value is readable by any local administrator and exposes the AutoLogon account's credentials in clear text.

Even when AutoLogon itself is intended (e.g. a kiosk), the password should never be left as a registry value. The Sysinternals Autologon tool stores the secret as an encrypted LSA secret precisely to avoid this; a plaintext `DefaultPassword` usually means a script or manual edit wrote the credentials directly.

Note: the agent only reports the *presence* of this value — it never reads or transmits the password itself.

remediation.title: Remove the plaintext password
steps: Delete the DefaultPassword value under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon
If AutoLogon is still required, reconfigure it with the Sysinternals Autologon tool so the secret is stored as an encrypted LSA secret rather than a registry value
Rotate the exposed account's password, since it may have been readable on disk

remediation.title: Reduce blast radius
steps: Verify the AutoLogon account is the least-privileged account that satisfies the use case (not a domain or local admin where avoidable)
For kiosk scenarios, prefer Assigned Access / kiosk profiles over a raw AutoLogon credential

relatedDocs.title: Sysinternals Autologon (encrypted LSA-secret password storage)

rules.title: AutoLogon enabled for an unexpected user
rules.description: Optional kiosk allow-list for AutoLogon. Fires when AutoLogon is enabled for a default user that is NOT on your configured list of approved kiosk / shared-device accounts. Disabled until configured because a normal Autopilot enrollment leaves Windows' own ESP auto-logon for the enrolling user (AutoLogon enabled, a default user, no plaintext password) — so AutoLogon-enabled alone is not graded by default; only a plaintext DefaultPassword on disk (ANALYZE-SEC-003) is. Enable this rule with your expected kiosk account name(s) if you want to surface AutoLogon configured for any other user.
rules.explanation:
AutoLogon is enabled on this device for a user that is **not on your approved kiosk allow-list**. AutoLogon is legitimate for kiosk and shared-device scenarios, so this rule only warns when the configured default user differs from the account(s) you expect.

This rule is **off by default**. A normal user-driven Autopilot enrollment leaves Windows' own ESP auto-logon for the enrolling user (`AutoAdminLogon=1`, a `DefaultUserName`, an `AutoLogonSID`, and no plaintext password) — registry-indistinguishable from a kiosk AutoLogon — so AutoLogon-enabled alone is not graded. The only AutoLogon state graded by default is a plaintext `DefaultPassword` on disk (**ANALYZE-SEC-003**).

To use this rule: configure the allow-list with your approved kiosk account name(s) (comma-separated, case-insensitive exact match against the Winlogon `DefaultUserName`) and enable it. AutoLogon for any other user will then warn.

Note: an AutoLogon with no explicit default user (e.g. ForceAutoLogon only) is treated as unexpected and will warn.

remediation.title: Confirm the AutoLogon user
steps: Open the autologon_analysis event in the session timeline and check the default user/domain
If the user is an approved kiosk account, add it to this rule's allow-list
If the user is unexpected, treat it as a possible enrollment/OOBE manipulation and investigate what wrote the Winlogon keys

remediation.title: Disable AutoLogon if not required
steps: Set HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon\AutoAdminLogon to 0
Remove DefaultPassword (and any Sysinternals LSA secret) if present
For kiosk scenarios, prefer Assigned Access / kiosk profiles over raw AutoLogon where possible

relatedDocs.title: Microsoft: Configure Windows to automatically sign in (AutoLogon)

templateVariables.name: allowed_autologon_users
templateVariables.label: Allowed AutoLogon users (kiosk accounts)
templateVariables.description: Comma-separated list of approved kiosk / shared-device account names that are expected to use AutoLogon (case-insensitive exact match against the Winlogon DefaultUserName, e.g. 'KioskUser' or 'Reception,LobbyPC'). AutoLogon for any other user raises a warning.

rules.title: Unexpected Provisioning Package
rules.description: Detects provisioning packages (PPKG) that are not on the built-in allow-list. The agent emits one or more provisioning_package_scan events whose artifacts[] arrays list every PPKG (registry package, .ppkg file, or Recovery\Customizations residue; a large set is split across several events). This rule iterates that array across all of them and fires for any artifact whose identity does not match the allow-list regex. The default allow-list covers the Windows OS-inbox packages (Cosa, Power.EnergyEstimationEngine, Power.Settings, SecureStart) plus common OEM factory-preload families (Intel PPM power profiles, AMD Power profiles, Lenovo ThinkPad power/battery packages, Microsoft Surface defaults and carrier packages) so clean factory devices stay quiet. OEM imaging leftovers (e.g. USMT.PPKG, InstallOrder.ini) and generically named packages deliberately still alert — on a pure-Autopilot device they are worth a look. To allow your own vendor/recovery packages, enable the template rule ANALYZE-SEC-006, extend its allow-list, then disable this rule.
rules.explanation:
A provisioning package (PPKG) was detected that is NOT on the allow-list.

- **Unexpected package:** `{{identity}}`

The agent reports every PPKG it finds in `provisioning_package_scan` event(s) (see their `artifacts` array(s) for the full list, plus file metadata and best-effort content indicators; large sets span several events). This rule ignores the Windows OS-inbox packages (Cosa, Power.EnergyEstimationEngine, Power.Settings, SecureStart) and the common OEM factory-preload families (Intel PPM power profiles, AMD Power profiles, Lenovo ThinkPad power/battery packages, Surface defaults and carrier packages) and only surfaces the rest. OEM imaging leftovers such as `USMT.PPKG` or `InstallOrder.ini` are deliberately NOT allow-listed — on a device expected to be freshly provisioned via Autopilot they indicate the image was touched by another deployment workflow.

Provisioning packages can apply local accounts, certificates, Wi-Fi/VPN profiles, applications (MSI/EXE) and scripts. They are legitimate in bulk-enrollment and OEM-recovery scenarios, but a PPKG appearing on a device that is expected to be "pure Autopilot" can indicate tampering.

**To stop alerting on a known-good package:** enable the template rule **ANALYZE-SEC-006**, add the package to its allow-list regex, then disable this rule. ANALYZE-SEC-006 then only fires for packages outside your extended allow-list.

remediation.title: Verify the provisioning package is expected
steps: Open the provisioning_package_scan event and review the artifacts array (identity, source, file path, ownerType/installTime for registry packages)
Confirm whether the package matches a known source: bulk-enrollment PPKG, OEM recovery package, or an internal provisioning workflow
Cross-check the device model/vendor — OEM recovery PPKGs are common and usually benign
If the package is unexpected for a pure-Autopilot device, treat it as a potential manipulation and investigate how it was applied (USB, Recovery\Customizations, image)
To allow known-good packages going forward, enable ANALYZE-SEC-006, extend the allow-list, then disable this rule

rules.title: Unexpected Provisioning Package (Custom Allow-List)
rules.description: Template version of ANALYZE-SEC-005 with a customizable allow-list. Iterates the provisioning_package_scan artifacts[] array and fires for any PPKG whose identity is not on YOUR allow-list regex. The placeholder ships with the same OS-inbox defaults as ANALYZE-SEC-005 — extend it with your own vendor/recovery packages. This rule is OFF by default: enable it, set your allow-list, then disable ANALYZE-SEC-005 so only packages outside your list alert.
rules.explanation:
A provisioning package (PPKG) was detected whose identity is NOT on your configured allow-list.

- **Unexpected package:** `{{identity}}`

The `identity` value combines a package's name, .ppkg file name, ownerType and packageId, so your allow-list regex can match an approved package by any of these. The default allow-list already covers the Windows OS-inbox packages (Cosa, Power.EnergyEstimationEngine, Power.Settings, SecureStart) and the common OEM factory-preload families (Intel PPM, AMD Power, Lenovo ThinkPad power/battery, Surface defaults and carrier packages) — extend it with your own vendor/recovery packages.

If this package should be allowed, add it to the allow-list regex (the 'value' of the 'ppkg_not_allowlisted' condition). If it is genuinely unexpected on a pure-Autopilot device, treat it as a potential manipulation and investigate how it was applied.

remediation.title: Triage the unexpected package
steps: Open the provisioning_package_scan event and review the artifacts array for this package's identity and source
Decide: is this a known-good package that should be allow-listed, or genuinely unexpected?

If known-good: the allow-list is anchored (^(?:...)\b), so insert a new alternative INSIDE the group, before the closing ')' — e.g. ...|SecureStart\.Settings|Acme\.Recovery)\b. Appending after the ) or \b will not match.
If unexpected: investigate the application path (USB media, C:\Recovery\Customizations residue, custom image) and the device's chain of custody

remediation.title: Activate the custom allow-list workflow (2-step)
steps: Enable this rule and extend the allow-list regex by inserting your approved packages as alternatives inside the anchored group (before the closing ')'), case-insensitive
Verify it behaves as expected on a known device
Disable the built-in rule ANALYZE-SEC-005 so only packages outside your allow-list alert going forward

templateVariables.name: allowed_packages
templateVariables.label: Allowed provisioning packages (regex)
templateVariables.description: Case-insensitive regex matched against each package's identity (name | fileName | ownerType | packageId). Packages matching this pattern are treated as approved and do NOT alert. The default is anchored (^...) so it matches only at the START of the identity — extend it by adding an alternative inside the group, e.g. add '|Acme\.Recovery' before the closing ')'.

rules.title: Provisioning Package Scan Incomplete
rules.description: Fires when the provisioning-package scan hit a per-source collection cap (too many .ppkg files, registry packages, or Recovery\Customizations residue files to capture them all). When that happens the allow-list rules (ANALYZE-SEC-005/006) could not evaluate every package, so a non-allow-listed package may have gone unseen. A device with that many provisioning packages is itself unusual and worth a manual look.
rules.explanation:
The provisioning-package scan was incomplete: the device had more provisioning packages than the agent captures per source, so a per-source cap was hit (see `ppkgFilesTruncated` / `packagesTruncated` / `recoveryFilesTruncated` on the `provisioning_package_scan` event).

Because not every package was captured, the allow-list rules (ANALYZE-SEC-005/006) may NOT have evaluated all of them — a non-allow-listed package could be present but unseen. A device carrying this many provisioning packages is also unusual on its own and warrants a manual review.

remediation.title: Review the device manually
steps: Open the provisioning_package_scan event and check which counts exceeded the cap (ppkgFileCount / packageCount vs the *Truncated flags)
Inspect the device directly: C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\Provisioning, C:\Windows\Provisioning, C:\Recovery\Customizations, and HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Provisioning\Packages
Confirm whether the large number of provisioning packages is expected for this device/model (e.g. an OEM image) or anomalous
If anomalous, treat it as a potential manipulation and investigate the device's chain of custody
